text:
Sports fans may be taken aback to discover thatOlympicgold hasn't always been the ultimate prize in the Games. After the Olympics were revived in 1896, silver medals were initially given to champions.

In 1904, however, they swapped silver for gold and haven't looked back. That said, gold medals haven't been made entirely of the substance since 1912. This makes sense given that each 529-gram medal would cost a whopping £30,000 to produce.

The medals on offer in Paris, valued at around £750, have just six grams of gold in them. The rest, ironically, is made out of silver, accounting for around 95 per cent of the medallion.

When these medals are won, athletes have developed a habit of biting them while posing for photos. In the past, it was said this tradition stemmed from merchants who used to bite gold coins to test their purity (gold is a soft metal, so if teeth leave an indent the coin is pure), but this theory has since been debunked.

David Wallechinsky, president of the International Society of Olympic Historians, revealed in 2012 the actual reason why athletes seem eager to taste the medal they've worked so hard to win. "It's become an obsession with the photographers," he told CNN.

"I think they look at it as an iconic shot, as something that you can probably sell. I don't think it's something the athletes would probably do on their own."



While the origin of athletes biting their medals remains unclear, it's known that photographers often encourage them to do so for a memorable shot. This iconic pose, coupled with an athlete's newfound fame, makes for a perfect photo opportunity.

Tom Daley, Team GB's celebrated diver, has amassed a collection of medals throughout his career, including three bronze, one gold, and one silver. He was seen obligingly biting his silver medal at this year's Games after finishing second in the 10m synchro diving final, thus completing his set of all three colours.

A silver medal is slightly lighter than a gold one and costs half as much to produce. Although the price of silver varies, a second-place medal would cost approximately £380.

A bronze medal, made mostly of copper, reportedly costs just over £10 to make but its value to those who earn it is immeasurable. Observers may have spotted an iron hexagon at the centre of each medal. This design element, introduced at the Paris Games, was inspired by France's nickname, L'Hexagone. The material for the hexagon was sourced from a piece of the iconic Eiffel Tower.

title: People are only just realising why Olympic stars bite their medals | Other | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Harry Brent
description: Olympic athletes have a habit of biting their medals while posing for photos on the podium. Here, we examine the reason behind this peculiar tradition.
